item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Sad Sad Tale of Dr. Harold Thompson.

Glad you enjoyed the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The feels, dude! This was so well written! I was reading along aloud my eyes glued to my phone! Doctor to SCP is a very interesting concept and this was executed SO well. AAAAA HAROLDDDDDD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The last interview was honestly a bit eye-rolling for me, with how hard it hammered in the euthanasia plot thread. The rest of the article was a well-built scp, though, with skillful escalation that doesn't feel unearned.

item.title: Re: Euthanasing Foundation
item.content:encoded:
Also the fact that there was no test log at all to back up this scp.

I mean, the document implies that there is plenty of testing going on with SCP-2860, what with a solid list of marble types produced, materials not affected by the effect, and high-speed photography being used to determine how the transformations take place.

A test log is not included in the document provided on the site because a) I felt it was not necessary for the narrative being told (not every article has a test log) and b) such a test log would be fairly boring, as it would be different flavors of "SCP-2860 touches X. X becomes/doesn't become marble."

These articles are designed to be entertaining snapshots of the Foundation, after all. Don't confuse artistic decisions out of universe for negligence in-universe.

As for why the Foundation doesn't destroy SCP-2860, well that's because that is not what they do. There are boatloads of SCPs that cause as much suffering/pain to the SCPs as SCP-2860 does, and are arguably more dangerous to have under lock and key.

If you don't like the portrayal of the Foundation here, that's fine. Downvote as you please. But you have a very long list of articles to hand a -1 if this is your reasoning for that.

item.title: Re: Euthanasing Foundation
item.content:encoded: The ethics committee should of stopped this by now. Clear suffering and no results. Also the fact that there was no test log at all to back up this scp. The smart thing would be to terminate before it gets worse and just the dust can change you. Or it starts changing inorganic and the guys in suits become statues.

item.title: Re: Euthanasing Foundation
item.content:encoded:
Apologies for the necro, but this is an issue that comes up more often than I'd like to see in SCPs, and I think it's worth continuing to bring attention to.

We know the lines well. "The Foundation is cold, not cruel." (There's a fine line between the two, though.) "It's Secure, Contain, Protect, not Destroy, Destroy, Destroy." (AKA the GOC.) But it's one thing to have the lines, another thing to make them work in practice. I think the main problem I see with some SCPs is that they take these to their conclusion without understanding why they exist.

And I think this is a good example. "The Foundation isn't in the habit of destroying anomalies." Right, but this isn't just callous destruction, this is a question of suffering. It's very clear 2860 is suffering, to the point where he's being put in a medically-induced coma to avoid him killing himself. (So he's not going to be giving any more verbal insights into his anomaly - not that there'd be anything to give after a decade.) That would qualify under many definitions as well into the "cruel" arena.

Now, this could be justified if there was reason to believe that the Foundation was gaining key information about the anomaly and making significant progress towards something. Except…where's the products of this decade of research? What have they learned that would justify keeping him alive? They have several months of tests, WHAT ARE THEY TESTING? If the goal is insight into anomalies, what insight have they gained? And that ends up being a fundamental problem I see a lot in these "we keep the anomaly alive against their will" works - we rarely if ever see anything to justify that. No scientific breakthroughs, no understanding of the anomalies, no production at all, just years of torture just because. That is NOT "cold, not cruel." That's blatant, bald-faced cruelty.

To justify avoiding the termination of an anomaly that wants to be terminated, there has to be something concrete behind it. Testing, information gain, something that justifies the actions and is demonstrably productive (not just testing for the sake of testing). Without that, the takeway becomes "Foundation are monsters" and I don't know what purpose that serves for anyone.

item.title: Re: Wikiwalk Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Sounds good to me.

item.title: Wikiwalk Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
edit: edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ hide
- show

To link to an article with a similar plot point, I propose the following edit:

Due to SCP-2860’s declining mental state and increasingly frequent attempts at suicide, the subject has been placed in a medically induced coma.

to

Due to SCP-2860’s declining mental state and increasingly frequent attempts at suicide, the subject has been placed in a medically induced coma according to the guidelines set by the Palmer Protocol.

item.title: Euthanasing Foundation
item.content:encoded:
This is for sure not the first SCP that portrays Foundation denying euthanasia. It's just the way they go; they don't provide nor grant commodities until needed to fulfill their role. Euthanasia is part of that set. Why don't they kill the girl that bends reality? They even had to apply to get permission to kill the damn omnicidal lizard!

Author had the detail to even change the interviewer for someone evidently less related to Thompson (he treats him with its designation number) to acknowledge that the friendlier doctor probably couldn't bear it. That's fine writing there, not "slogan adhesion".

As for the "slogan", is part of the very first guideline you have to completely read in order to become a member. it's not a slogan. It's not even canon. It's METAcanon, part of how this site is thought about. The fact that this is +96 somewhat shows you're playing offbeat with this argument.

And last but not least, "stupid" is an adjective that adds nothing to discussion and needlessly makes one want to report you. Don't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For me this is a nice departure from the standard scientific detachment mentality usually found in these reports. Of course good science needs the researcher to stand apart from the subject researched, but scientists, even (most) Foundation scientists, are human. It is that humanity, and empathy, that is one of our species greatest strengths.

I am not saying that all SCP entry's should be like this, but the occasional reminder that science can have 'real' world consequences is important for contrast. Well done, +1

item.title: Re: A question
item.content:encoded: Even from yourself. :P

item.title: Re: A question
item.content:encoded: No worries. The tag police are here to protect you. ;)

item.title: Re: A question
item.content:encoded: Right on. Makes sense.

item.title: Re: A question
item.content:encoded: You're confusing mind-affecting with cognitohazardous.

item.title: Re: A question
item.content:encoded:
And I've reinstated it. It's tagged cognitohazard because while a very literal reading of the tag's description might imply that 'observed' was meant purely visual, it's actually meant as 'recorded by any of the 5 senses', hence the instruction to pair it with auditory, gustatory, olfactory, tactile or visual.

In this case, it's a tactile cognitohazard, as activation occurs upon physical contact.

item.title: Re: A question
item.content:encoded:
I actually have no idea.

That tag was added during the initial taging, by the tagging staff at the time.

Its been removed, since the tag guide definition of cognitohazard is "SCP is hazardous to subjects when observed" which is not really fit in this case.

item.title: A question
item.content:encoded: I like this, but… why is this tagged "cognitohazard"? Because the skip might convince other people to kill it?

item.title: Re: Love this one.
item.content:encoded:
Not a missing verb, but an unnecessary "to".

Good catch.

item.title: Love this one.
item.content:encoded:
I've always loved transfiguration skips, and the fact that you made me feel so much for this one is a testament to your writing skill. Well done sir.
There appears to be a missing verb in the third paragraph of the description:

…individual microscopic organisms present on SCP-2860’s skin to undergo transformation upon exposure.

It seems like there should be a verb between "skin" and "to."
Sorry. I'm a bit of a stickler when it comes to grammar.

item.title: Re: Chilled
item.content:encoded:
Thanks.

Dr. Thompson is a pretty tragic character Peregrine but the goal of this article was to really set that in stone.

item.title: Chilled
item.content:encoded: Reading through this I got a chill. I first encountered him in a few tales but damn… this hit home. 1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh.

You're the first person to bring the similarity of this with 1707 to my attention.

Oh well. We definitely have articles that share tropes on this site (an example being how many ghost ships of various flavors can be found on here), so 2860 and 1707 both sharing a theme of containment destroying the skip is not what I would consider a deal breaker.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I will need to read the tales and then reread this, but it recently struck me that this is thematically very close (though not quite a rehash) to SCP-1707. They're different characters and their "progress" is different, but they both ride on the same basic trope of containment destroying them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not very fair to say that the Foundation never learns anything from SCPs. Each article is only a snapshot in time, so of course they're going to contain a lot of works in process. But we have a several Neutralised articles containing things that are considered basic science now, but were once perceived as supernatural. I consider that evidence that the Foundation does sometimes come to completely understand an SCP, and then that knowledge simply because science. For those whose headcanon suggests that the Foundation has more advanced science that we know, the reason they have access to such advanced technology is because of their analysis and limited understanding of various SCPs. Even basic things like meme-kill agents or amnestics were probably developed from SCPs.

So there is absolutely a point to keeping SCPs alive if they can. There's no telling if, in a month, there's going to be a breakthrough in this technology or another that allows us to obtain more understanding of him or help alleviate his condition. Not to mention the article mentions that they have months' worth of tests lined up for him—there's no telling what kind of results those tests might produce. An SCP is almost always more useful alive than dead, unless it's actually currently threatening the fate of the world.

And that's why they didn't kill him as soon as he requested. Because if they had destroyed every SCP as soon as somebody felt like giving up, they wouldn't have discovered half the stuff they did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As I said in chat, I'm happy with the adjustments you've made.1 I'm also pleased with you switching to collapsibles for the revisions, as it looks much cleaner to me. And having the addendum interview logs with bolded titles before the collapsibles is rather snazzy looking; I may co-opt that.

What's still odd to me, from an in-universe perspective, is that the visible Cont. Proc. are the oldest version. From a storytelling point of view I know this makes sense for the sake of linearity but if this were a real report it would have the most recent update visible with a revision history beneath. There's no right answer to this, per se, so I'm not actually saying to change it; I just found it worth noting.

Footnotes
1. Also, I totally looked at the revision history and saw that the coma bit was added on the 11th; before my 2096 went mainlist but after I'd drafted it with that element, so yay for me not having accidentally stolen the idea from you! ^_^

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've tired to clean up the dialogue here and there to make it somewhat less shoddy (especially with the "you of all people" bits.)

Thanks for the feedback, even if the end product wasn't your cup of tea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Transitioning elements from tales to articles is a bit of tricky business. What may be a perfectly serviceable character/object in a tale may end up feeling bland or incomplete when written as an SCP. Unfortunately I feel that's what happened here. The somewhat shoddy dialogue doesn't really help matters (especially the usage of "you of all people" twice in the interview logs). Sorry, but I'm not too keen on this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Instead of making the foundation change its behavior, you had the researcher obviously hanging on to that stupid slogan to avoid feeling responsibility for the terrible things he's a party to.

That's fucking fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, for starters, all of the tales written about SCP-2860 so far take place before the more advanced stages of the anomaly, during which time Dr. Freemont was in charge.

In the most recent version of this skip, Dr. Cassidy does offer to help with the situation, but for the large part this is some towing of the party line.

item.title: Good job
item.content:encoded: Upvoted :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That purpose is protecting human life from supernatural threats.

No, that is the party line. The Foundation (at least the one in my headcanon) doesn't give a shit about Humanity, just about keeping themselves in operation for some twisted reason only the O5's really know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you want to destroy anomalous things just 'cause they're anomalous, you go to the GOC.

I want the people charged with protecting humanity from the anomalous to be motivated by protecting humanity from the anomalous, instead of being motivated by adhering to a stupid slogan.

If they keep 2860 alive he can explain what happens to him when he causes things to be transmuted into marble and possibly gain pertinent information

They are not trying to do this and would never succeed at doing this if they were.

what if they could cure him?

They are not trying to do this and would never succeed at doing this if they were AND if for some reason they did succeed, they would still keep him confined indefinitely because Hey Man You Never Know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm digging it. This is absolutely an improvement, and I liked it from the get-go.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've added some new content and done several tweaks to the existing content. Hopefully this addresses some of the issues some of you have pointed out. I do feel that this version is improved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you want to destroy anomalous things just 'cause they're anomalous, you go to the GOC. If they keep 2860 alive he can explain what happens to him when he causes things to be transmuted into marble and possibly gain pertinent information - It could be very helpful if he could describe the "small static discharge" he feels in more detail, for example. Besides, the fact that 2860 got the anomaly from that rod proves that its possible to transfer the effect - what if they could cure him?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"It's Secure, Contain, Protect not Destroy, Destroy, Destroy" is not an argument, it's a slogan, and it's not a particularly good one.

Securing, Containing and Protecting is not something that is undertaken for its own benefit; the things they do must be for SOME PURPOSE. That purpose is protecting human life from supernatural threats. Most of the time they do this by containing things. If they could do this by destroying something, and someone said "We can't do this! We're not the Destroy, Destroy, Destroy Foundation!" that person would be beaten unconscious with a pool cue.

And they AREN'T studying him. They are gaining no useful information from him. There is nothing to suggest they are attempting to gain useful information from him in the article, and on top of this, they NEVER gain any useful information from ANY SCP, ever, that has any application to anything beyond the containment of the SCP itself, because The SCP Has To Be Mysterious. (This is part of what makes them screamingly incompetent, how much science they can try to do while still learning nothing.)

And they don't know what his corpse will do? They don't know what ANYTHING will do EVER. Their total and fundamental incompetence at gaining information should render them incapable of any action, because when you have no idea what any of the rules are (and they have no idea what any of the rules are), all actions or lack of actions have the same chance of a terrible outcome. They don't know what he'll do when he's dead, but they don't know what he'll do when he stays alive tomorrow either. Since Bad Shit Happening follows no rules whatsoever, no action or lack of action can reduce the risk of Bad Shit Happening. If all actions have equal chances of catastrophe, take the action that is faster, cheaper, and doesn't involve torturing someone.

The fact the guy's condition is disintegrating and there's no tests yielding any information suggests they are not attempting to learn this information; as the condition is clearly killing him tortuously, if they cared about learning things from him, they'd be expediting that shit to get it done before he became unusable. They are not. There is nothing to indicate they are attempting to do so.

They are allowing a guy, a guy who the employees personally known, to be slowly and agonizingly tortured for no in-universe reason whatsoever, and the only out-of-universe reason is to allow the author to adhere to a stupid slogan.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
how Dr. Thompson affected the release of the particulate

This is actually one of those rare times when you want "effect" as a verb. 'Tis fixeth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, but I'm curious about something.

I haven't read the tales yet, but based on the SCP alone: Does Dr. Cassidy have any ulterior motivation, or is he just toeing the SCP party line? I completely understand that the Foundation as a whole wouldn't kill him (for many good reasons iterated above), that researchers can't let SCPs dictate their containment, and that the Ethics Committee would have plenty of reasons to deny such a request. On the other hand, there's also no reason a researcher couldn't kick such a request up the line, if they so desired. I'm just curious if I'm missing a personal element.

Edit: Sorry, I meant this to be a new response.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like I would need to read all of the relevant Tales to really appreciate this, and I haven't done so as of yet. So no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't recall reading any tales about this guy. This is humanoid SCP done more or less right, though. I mean, I'll give you an upvote just for "anart ruins lives".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the tales he's in, but this seems to only serve as a supplement to those tales. On its own, it feels uninteresting and the entirety of the article seems to be riding on the interview logs to flesh out the character. If you take this article by itself, with no context, this is a pretty uninteresting character. The trope of "researcher turned into an anomaly" IS genuinely interesting to me. However, I feel that this isn't done well enough to sustain interest, and only serves as a way for those who like the tales to learn more about the character, which I can't really endorse. You could argue that on its own it could stand fine, but the nature of the article conveniently feels like it leaves out too much to get fully invested in the character or the nature of the anomaly, and you can't learn more without checking out the tales.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This, and the Foundation's motto is Secure, Contain, Protect, not Destroy, Destroy, Destroy. With very few exceptions, the Foundation isn't in the business of destroying SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's the Secure, Contain, Protect Foundation, not the Destroy, Destroy, Destroy Foundation. Anomalous things, by their nature, have information that can be gained from them. If they completely understood him, they would slap an -EX on the end of his SCP designation and then maybe kill him. Since there is still some information to be gained from him, they're keeping him alive to study how his anomalous effects affect him. For example, they might want to look into that "feeling of a small static discharge." that he gets when he converts things - you can't interview him when he's dead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This SCP is basically an origin story for one of Conwell's established characters. Quite frankly, this isn't my favorite use of the character, but it's neat for future readers of the relevant Tales to be able to know right away who the character is.

Actually, upon further consideration, I'll say this: I want to see more interviews and other reports on the SCP so it's not as propped up by the Tales as it is now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We don't know what'll happen to his corpse. We know what he does alive. What he'll do dead is unknown.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ..Why DON'T they kill him, though? It's not like they derive any benefit or information from his continued life. It seems like they are being pointlessly cruel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2860 is to be contained within a Type S Standard Humanoid Anomaly Containment Cell at Site-19.

Right off the start, I love it. No needing to pester the reader with exacting details, or how long the bed is, or the GPF of the toilet. Just a quick, simple setup, informative enough to know what's being held and the accommodations we have for the item, but leaving enough to the reader's imagination so they paint their own picture of a Standard Humanoid Anomaly Containment Cell. It provides us the palette, paint, and brush, to let us design and visualize our personal version of this particular part of the SCP Foundation.

I feel this should be done more in general, for items that don't require an exactly defined containment location for adequate containment. Too often I see all these unnecessarily precise measurements and, often asinine detail put into containment procedures. The idea of the Standard Containment Cell (for humanoids, or documents, or electronic equipment, or whatever) helps keep clutter out of articles, and helps draw the reader in, since they're populating their own mental version of the SCP Foundation, instead of a strictly dictated version of the writer.

Anyways, this is getting weirdly off track. The SCP is great - I love the trope of Researcher Becoming Subject, and this is an interesting story written into a good SCP article

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kind of not feeling this one for some reason.

It basically is a person that got exposed to something and now has a superhero-like power that he cannot control (which is a very generic idea in my opinion).

Sure, there is some character development and somewhat of a story, but it is all rather predictable. I expected him to deteriorate mentally as time went on, and I was proven right. I think some sort of screw that most people were not expecting would grealty improve this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not as silly as you might think. Yule marble.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a somewhat silly question, but what kind of marble does he make?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Couldn't they be put in a medically induced coma or one of those supposedly "germ-free" cells or whatever in order to provide them some amount of relief? Pain medication? etc?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fixed a couple little grammar things:

+ show block
– hide block

currently unknown due its immediate disappearance after release.

Added "to".

must be fed intravenously to due to food undergoing transformation during all attempts of SCP-2860 to feed itself.

Removed a "to".

I like this but I think it needs one more draft/revision. The repeated references to the subject by name in the third paragraph don't quite work for me, even given the familiarity of the subject and the staff. There's also a couple spots like

An isolation suit has been provided to provide relief.

that feel like they could be refined. Content-wise, I enjoyed most of it but nothing really struck me in particular. There's good feels-building but it's a no-vote for me right now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No lecture needed.
When you are right, you are right, and Class A has been switched to Class B

I feex gud.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, I appeared in an SCP! I guess I am cool now.
+1, nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I prefer the character as he appears in the Tales, but this is pretty damn good. I especially liked the escalation of the effect and acknowledgment of how it effects his health. My only issue is that Level B or C HAZMAT gear would probably cover it, but I don't feel like giving my HAZMAT lecture and will let it slide.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That last interview log..
I never asked for those feels

item.title: Opening Post
item.content:encoded:
Something I was working on over Winter Break. Posting now before the new term of college takes away all my free time.

Not sure how often we have something go from tale to article, rather than article to tale, but we have at least one example of that now.

Special thanks to TwistedGears, Randomini, kaktus, Crayne and Dr. Leonard for looking this over and raking it over the coals.

For those of you interested, the poor Dr. Thompson first appeared in the following tales:
Last Meetings
Building Up
Tearing Down
No One Should Be Alone

Enjoy the thing.
